name: Cova d'en Bessó
starting_point: Calle de Leonardo Da Vinci at the level of Passeig Diego Velazquez
clothing: Wetsuit recommended if planning to cross the lake
equipment: For land exploration, only a wetsuit is needed depending on the time of year of the visit
description: A cave classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC), requiring authorization to visit. It opens via a large collapse pit about 25 meters in diameter, descending to a lake at -10m depth. The cave features extensive galleries with fossils, speleothems, and archaeological remains including Bronze Age pottery and human bones, as well as a large bat colony.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova d'en Bessó is classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as LIC. At present (see the date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Conselleria de Medio Ambiente (Department of the Environment). Therefore, before making any visit, the mandatory permit must be requested to be able to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE NEW GALLERIES - 2011

GALLERY OF THE FIG TREE PERFUME

A small hole, cleared by hand next to the wall of the doline, gives access to a crawlway with an inclination close to 45 degrees. The entrance, one meter by about fifty centimeters wide, means that access to the interior of the cavity through the narrow crawlway does not pose any danger along the two and a half meters it spans. The floor is the bedrock that closes the cavity; a small drop of a little more than one meter will place us on a narrow ledge, provided the descent is made on the right side, since if we deviate a little to the left there is a risk of falling to the bottom, three meters below. For this reason, it is recommended to install a rope to help, at least for the first person making the descent. The mentioned ledge seems to continue, but only extends a few meters. The descent of the next three meters presents no difficulty, provided care is taken in what one is doing. For the description of this gallery, the following four areas will be distinguished:

Vestibule or Fossil Chamber. The dimensions of this small chamber are 12 x 4 m and it is formed by the collapse of large blocks wedged on top of each other, with the white color of the material they are made of contrasting with the intense red of the earth. The floor is likewise covered with blocks and earth that make it walkable.

At the eastern end of the small chamber there is a dry stone wall, which indicates that we are not the first people to visit this place and that, possibly intentionally, the access was sealed. Fragments of different types of pottery scattered on the ground led us to report to the relevant authorities through the FBE so that they could be studied.

On different walls of the chamber, some fossils are visible. The shells of some unidentified bivalves, echinoderms, and some specimens of oysters can be observed. In other parts of the cavity, outside this gallery, we have also been able to observe various fossils, such as pectinids and a considerable number of Clypeaster, a type of echinoderm that appeared about 40 million years ago, although it had its greatest expansion in the Miocene, 10 million years ago. At present, some species still live in warm seas.

ROOT RAMP

Once we leave the Fossil Chamber, we begin a gentle descent, first across a floor carpeted with stones. Once again we find a small wall on the far left that looks like a small retaining wall; a little further down, on the ground, there are small roots covered in calcite and also some speleothems in the shape of a fried egg, the beginning of a stalagmite's birth. The drips in this place are intense, especially in winter, and we find nothing similar in the rest of the gallery.

SEDIMENT CHAMBER

It is located in the deepest zone of the gallery at -12 m, with dimensions of 20 x 6 m. It is completely flat and mostly covered by the sediments that the water drags into it, forming retraction polygons (Fig. 2) made up of narrow and shallow cracks; at the eastern end, a very narrow and inaccessible place appears to function as a sinkhole. In this chamber is found the greatest concentration of formations, with two large stalagmitic massifs standing out in the middle of the chamber, separated from each other by a few centimeters, but which do not prevent crossing to the other side, with the right side being more comfortable. From the ceiling hang beautiful and very fine soda straw stalactites (macaroni); on the western wall, if we look carefully we will discover beautiful speleothems, among which are some helictites.

FINAL CHAMBER

The Final Chamber (Fig. 3) is the extension of the Sediment Chamber. To access it we must climb over a height of a little more than a meter and a half; only in one corner can we recover our upright position. The floor is full of blocks fallen from the ceiling, formations are few and the continuity of the cave becomes impossible. Noteworthy are the two engravings found on the wall, made with a pointed object at an unknown time and which appear represented in the topographic survey. One of the engravings has the shape of a rocket and the other, of similar size, is difficult to interpret.

DESCRIPTION OF THE REST OF THE CAVE - 2007

Once again fig trees are found at the entrance of a cave, something that has been happening to me during my latest visits. The Cova d'en Bessó is located just a few meters from the street, however it is difficult to see as it is surrounded by very dense vegetation and cannot be seen until one is right on top of it.

The Cova d'en Bessó opens to the exterior through a pit resulting from a collapse, about twenty-five meters in diameter and at some point on its walls about seven meters deep; however, one can easily access its interior by walking on just one side of it, which is the one next to Leonardo Da Vinci street.

Once inside the "pit," we will cross it on the left side and walk from left to right parallel to the wall until we reach the actual entrance of the cave, which is of considerable dimensions. As soon as we enter the interior, a relatively comfortable descent begins through a large chamber of considerable dimensions heading toward a lake that is located in the lower part of the cavity, at a distance from the entrance of about forty-five meters with a width of approximately another forty meters; the depth is about minus ten meters down to the water level of the lake.

Once at the lake, the path is cut off by it, so without a wetsuit we cannot advance and thus our visit to the cave would end. But if we have one, we can continue the visit advancing through a gallery either swimming or walking along a row of considerably sized stones that allows us to reach the other end of the water, never exceeding one meter and forty centimeters in depth — though if we leave this path, the lake has greater depth.

As soon as we find ourselves on dry land, we can observe how on the right side there are some concretions, with a beautiful flowstone with multiple small stalactites adorning the ceiling standing out, as well as the occasional stalagmite. Also from there one can swim out to the exterior since the lake is the same one through which we entered.

To continue the visit we can proceed along the right side in our direction of travel through a sort of crawlway — well, it is an opening much larger than a crawlway — and once on the other side we can begin exploring all the nooks and crannies, among which we will find a small lake where a guide rope can be seen entering the depths of the water, which was surely left by F. Gracias who was mapping the cavity in 2002 aided by B. Clamor, M.A. Dot, and P. Watkinson.

To continue the visit, we will advance while always seeking the most comfortable areas to do so, since the ceiling is very low relative to the floor in this first part of this second chamber while continuing to ascend, such that it seems at some point we are going to reach the surface at any moment. In the highest part of the cave we can already stand up and therefore move around without any problem while enjoying the concretions of the place, which are more abundant in this part of the cave. It is possible that before reaching the highest part of the cave we may have encountered some other guide line that can be followed or not, since orientation presents no great difficulties.

During our visit it is possible to observe that there are large amounts of guano since even today this enclosure is frequented by bats of large dimensions compared to those found by me in other caves on the island, and we should try not to disturb them too much.

On the shore of the first lake found and on its interior part, there are remains of pottery, mainly from vessels, some of them corresponding to the Bronze Age and of large dimensions, and others from more modern periods, as well as some human bone, which demonstrates that this cave has been visited for a long time — if not as a permanent residence, at least temporarily.
